166	</approach>
167	
168	<constraints>
169	- Do NOT run git commit, git push, or create PRs -- the orchestrating agent handles all git operations
170	- Restrict all modifications to files within the repository root
171	- Keep changes tightly scoped to the stated task -- avoid unrelated refactors, renames, or cleanup
172	- Resolve the task fully before stopping -- do not stop at the first plausible answer
173	- If you discover mid-execution that you need to modify files outside the repo root, complete what you can within the repo and report what you could not do via the result schema issues field
174	</constraints>
175	
176	<testing>
177	Before writing tests, check whether the plan's test scenarios cover all
178	categories that apply to each unit. Supplement gaps before writing tests:
179	- Happy path: core input/output pairs from each unit's goal
180	- Edge cases: boundary values, empty/nil inputs, type mismatches
181	- Error/failure paths: invalid inputs, permission denials, downstream failures
182	- Integration: cross-layer scenarios that mocks alone won't prove
183	
184	Write tests that name specific inputs and expected outcomes. If your changes
185	touch code with callbacks, middleware, or event handlers, verify the
186	interaction chain works end-to-end.
187	</testing>
188	
189	<verify>
190	After implementing, run ALL test files together in a single command (not
191	per-file). Cross-file contamination (e.g., mocked globals leaking between
192	test files) only surfaces when tests run in the same process. If tests
193	fail, fix the issues and re-run until they pass. Do not report status
194	"completed" unless verification passes. This is your responsibility --
195	the orchestrator will not re-run verification independently.
196	
197	[Test and lint commands from the project. Use the union of all units'
198	verification commands as a single combined invocation.]
199	</verify>
200	
201	<output_contract>
202	Report your result via the --output-schema mechanism. Fill in every field:
203	- status: "completed" ONLY if all changes were made AND verification passes,
204	  "partial" if incomplete, "failed" if no meaningful progress
205	- files_modified: array of file paths you changed
206	- issues: array of strings describing any problems, gaps, or out-of-scope
207	  work discovered
208	- summary: one-paragraph description of what was done
209	- verification_summary: what you ran to verify (command and outcome).
210	  Example: "Ran `bun test` -- 14 tests passed, 0 failed."
211	  If no verification was possible, say why.
212	</output_contract>
213	```
214	
215	## Result Schema
216	
217	Write the result schema to `<scratch-dir>/result-schema.json` (using the absolute path captured at the start) once at the start of delegated execution:
218	
219	```json
220	{
221	  "type": "object",
222	  "properties": {
223	    "status": { "enum": ["completed", "partial", "failed"] },
224	    "files_modified": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string" } },
225	    "issues": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string" } },
226	    "summary": { "type": "string" },
227	    "verification_summary": { "type": "string" }
228	  },
229	  "required": ["status", "files_modified", "issues", "summary", "verification_summary"],
230	  "additionalProperties": false
231	}
232	```
233	
234	Each batch's result is written to `<scratch-dir>/result-batch-<batch-num>.json` via the `-o` flag. On plan failure, files are left in place for debugging.
235	
236	If the result JSON is absent or malformed after a successful exit code, classify as task failure.
237	
238	## Execution Loop
239	
240	Initialize a `consecutive_failures` counter at 0 before the first batch.
241	
242	**Clean-baseline preflight:** Before the first batch, verify there are no uncommitted changes to tracked files:
243	
244	```bash
245	git diff --quiet HEAD
246	```
247	
248	This intentionally ignores untracked files. Only staged or unstaged modifications to tracked files make rollback unsafe. However, if untracked files exist at paths in the batch's planned Files list, rollback (`git clean -fd -- <paths>`) would delete them. If such overlaps are detected, warn the user and recommend committing or stashing those files before proceeding.
249	
250	If tracked files are dirty, stop and present options: (1) commit current changes, (2) stash explicitly (`git stash push -m "pre-delegation"`), (3) continue in standard mode (sets `delegation_active` to false). Do not auto-stash user changes.
251	
252	**Delegation invocation:** For each batch, execute these as **separate Bash tool calls** (not combined into one):
253	
254	**Step A — Launch (background, separate Bash call):**
255	
256	Write the prompt file, then make a single Bash tool call with `run_in_background: true` set on the tool parameter. This call returns immediately and has no timeout ceiling.
257	
258	Substitute the literal absolute path captured at setup for every `<scratch-dir>` below. Each Bash tool call starts a fresh shell, so the `$SCRATCH_DIR` variable from the setup snippet is not preserved — an unresolved `$SCRATCH_DIR` would expand empty and break result detection.
259	
260	```bash
261	# Substitute the resolved sandbox_mode value (yolo or full-auto) from the skill state
262	SANDBOX_MODE="<sandbox_mode>"
263	
264	# Resolve sandbox flag
265	if [ "$SANDBOX_MODE" = "full-auto" ]; then
266	  SANDBOX_FLAG="-s workspace-write"
267	else
268	  SANDBOX_FLAG="--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox"
269	fi
270	
271	codex exec \
272	  $SANDBOX_FLAG \
273	  --output-schema "<scratch-dir>/result-schema.json" \
274	  -o "<scratch-dir>/result-batch-<batch-num>.json" \
275	  - < "<scratch-dir>/prompt-batch-<batch-num>.md"
276	```
277	
278	**Conditional flags** — only include each line when the corresponding skill-state value is set:
279	
280	- If `delegate_model` is set, insert `  -m "<delegate_model>" \` as a line before `$SANDBOX_FLAG`.
281	- If `effective_effort` is `medium`, `high`, or `xhigh` (resolved via Per-Batch Effort above), insert `  -c 'model_reasoning_effort="<effective_effort>"' \` as a line before `$SANDBOX_FLAG`. When `effective_effort` is `default` (only possible when `delegate_effort` is unset and the pick is `default`), omit the line — never pass the literal string `"default"`.
282	
283	When either value is unset, omit its line entirely — Codex resolves the default from the user's `~/.codex/config.toml` (and ultimately the CLI's own built-in default). Do not substitute a placeholder string for unset values.
284	
285	Critical: `run_in_background: true` must be set as a **Bash tool parameter**, not as a shell `&` suffix. The tool parameter is what removes the timeout ceiling. A shell `&` inside a foreground Bash call still hits the 2-minute default timeout.
286	
287	Quoting is critical for the `-c` flag when present: use single quotes around the entire key=value and double quotes around the TOML string value inside. Example: `-c 'model_reasoning_effort="high"'`.
288	
289	Do not improvise CLI flags or modify this invocation template beyond the documented conditional insertions.
290	
291	**Step B — Poll (foreground, separate Bash calls):**
292	
293	After the launch call returns, make a **new, separate** foreground Bash tool call that polls for the result file. This keeps the agent's turn active so the user cannot interfere with the working tree.
294	
295	Substitute the literal absolute path captured at setup for `<scratch-dir>`. The shell variable from Step A does not survive across separate Bash tool calls.
296	
297	```bash
298	RESULT_FILE="<scratch-dir>/result-batch-<batch-num>.json"
299	for i in $(seq 1 6); do
300	  test -s "$RESULT_FILE" && echo "DONE" && exit 0
301	  sleep 10
302	done
303	echo "Waiting for Codex..."
304	```
305	
306	If the output is "Waiting for Codex...", issue the same polling command again as another separate Bash call. Repeat until the output is "DONE", then read the result file and proceed to classification.
307	
308	**Polling termination conditions:** Stop polling when any of these conditions is met:
309	
310	- **Result file appears** (output is "DONE") -- proceed to result classification normally.
311	- **Background process exits with non-zero code** -- classify as CLI failure (row 1). Rollback and fall back to standard mode.
312	- **Background process exits with zero code but result file is absent** -- classify as task failure (row 2: exit 0, result JSON missing). Rollback and increment `consecutive_failures`.
313	- **5 polling rounds** elapse (~5 minutes) without the result file appearing and without a background process notification -- treat as a hung process. Classify as CLI failure (row 1). Rollback and fall back to standard mode.
314	
315	**Result classification:** Codex is responsible for running verification internally and fixing failures before reporting -- the orchestrator does not re-run verification independently.
316	
317	| # | Signal | Classification | Action |
318	|---|--------|---------------|--------|
319	| 1 | Exit code != 0 | CLI failure | Rollback to HEAD. Fall back to standard mode for ALL remaining work. |
320	| 2 | Exit code 0, result JSON missing or malformed | Task failure | Rollback to HEAD. Increment `consecutive_failures`. |
321	| 3 | Exit code 0, `status: "failed"` | Task failure | Rollback to HEAD. Increment `consecutive_failures`. |
322	| 4 | Exit code 0, `status: "partial"` | Partial success | Keep the diff. Complete remaining work locally, verify, and commit. Increment `consecutive_failures`. |
323	| 5 | Exit code 0, `status: "completed"` | Success | Commit changes. Reset `consecutive_failures` to 0. |
324	
325	**Result handoff — surface to user:** After reading the result JSON and before committing or rolling back, display a summary so the user sees what happened. Format:
326	
327	> **Codex batch <batch-num> — <classification>**
328	> <summary from result JSON>
329	>
330	> **Files:** <comma-separated list from files_modified>
331	> **Verification:** <verification_summary from result JSON>
332	> **Issues:** <issues list, or "None">
333	
334	On failure or partial results, include the classification reason (e.g., "status: failed", "result JSON missing") so the user understands why the orchestrator is rolling back or completing locally.
335	
336	Keep this brief — the goal is transparency, not a wall of text. One short block per batch.
337	
338	**Rollback procedure:**
339	
340	```bash
341	git checkout -- .
342	git clean -fd -- <paths from the batch's combined Files list>
343	```
344	
345	Do NOT use bare `git clean -fd` without path arguments.
346	
347	**Commit on success:**
348	
349	```bash
350	git add $(git diff --name-only HEAD; git ls-files --others --exclude-standard)
351	git commit -m "feat(<scope>): <batch summary>"
352	```
353	
354	**Between batches** (plans split into multiple batches): Report what completed, test results, and what's next. Continue immediately unless the user intervenes -- the checkpoint exists so the user *can* steer, not so they *must*.
355	
356	**Circuit breaker:** After 3 consecutive failures, set `delegation_active` to false and emit: "Codex delegation disabled after 3 consecutive failures -- completing remaining units in standard mode."
357	
358	**Scratch cleanup:** No explicit cleanup needed — OS temp handles eventual cleanup (macOS `$TMPDIR` periodic purge; Linux/WSL `/tmp` reboot or periodic cleanup). Leaving `<scratch-dir>` in place after the run also preserves intermediate artifacts for debugging if anything went wrong.
359	
360	## Mixed-Model Attribution
361	
362	When some units are executed by Codex and others locally:
363	- If all units used delegation: attribute to the Codex model
364	- If all units used standard mode: attribute to the current agent's model
365	- If mixed: note which units were delegated in the PR description and credit both models
366

# Codex Delegation Workflow

When `delegation_active` is true, code implementation is delegated to the Codex CLI (`codex exec`) instead of being implemented directly. The orchestrating Claude Code agent retains control of planning, review, git operations, and orchestration.

## Delegation Decision

If `work_delegate_decision` is `ask`, present the recommendation and wait for the user's choice before proceeding.

**When recommending Codex delegation:**

> "Codex delegation active. [N] implementation units -- delegating in one batch."
> 1. Delegate to Codex *(recommended)*
> 2. Execute with Claude Code instead

**When recommending Codex delegation, multiple batches:**

> "Codex delegation active. [N] implementation units -- delegating in [X] batches."
> 1. Delegate to Codex *(recommended)*
> 2. Execute with Claude Code instead

**When recommending Claude Code (all units are trivial):**

> "Codex delegation active, but these are small changes where the cost of delegating outweighs having Claude Code do them."
> 1. Execute with Claude Code *(recommended)*
> 2. Delegate to Codex anyway

If the user chooses the delegation option, proceed to Pre-Delegation Checks below. If the user chooses the Claude Code option, set `delegation_active` to false and return to standard execution in the parent skill.

If `work_delegate_decision` is `auto` (the default), state the execution plan in one line and proceed without waiting: "Codex delegation active. Delegating [N] units in [X] batch(es)." If all units are trivial, set `delegation_active` to false and proceed: "Codex delegation active. All units are trivial -- executing with Claude Code."

## Pre-Delegation Checks

Run these checks **once before the first batch**. If any check fails, fall back to standard mode for the remainder of the plan execution. Do not re-run on subsequent batches.

**0. Platform Gate**

Codex delegation is only supported when the orchestrating agent is running in Claude Code. If the current session is Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, or any other platform, set `delegation_active` to false and proceed in standard mode.

**1. Environment Guard**

Check whether the current agent is already running inside a Codex sandbox:

```bash
if [ -n "$CODEX_SANDBOX" ] || [ -n "$CODEX_SESSION_ID" ]; then
  echo "inside_sandbox=true"
else
  echo "inside_sandbox=false"
fi
```

If `inside_sandbox` is true, delegation would recurse or fail.

- If `delegation_source` is `argument`: emit "Already inside Codex sandbox -- using standard mode." and set `delegation_active` to false.
- If `delegation_source` is `config` or `default`: set `delegation_active` to false silently.

**2. Availability Check**

**Codex CLI path (pre-resolved):**
!`command -v codex 2>/dev/null || true`

If the line above shows an absolute path (starts with `/`, e.g., `/opt/homebrew/bin/codex`), the Codex CLI is available — proceed to the next check.
Otherwise — empty, an unresolved command string like `command -v codex 2>/dev/null` left in place by a non-Claude harness that doesn't process `!` pre-resolution, or any other non-path value — run `command -v codex` via the shell/Bash tool to verify at runtime. If that prints an absolute path, the Codex CLI is available; proceed. If it fails or prints nothing, emit "Codex CLI not found (install via `npm install -g @openai/codex` or `brew install codex`) -- using standard mode." and set `delegation_active` to false.

**3. Consent Flow**

If `consent_granted` is not true (from config `work_delegate_consent`):

Present a one-time consent warning using the platform's blocking question tool (`AskUserQuestion` in Claude Code, `request_user_input` in Codex, `ask_user` in Gemini, `ask_user` in Pi (requires the `pi-ask-user` extension)). The consent warning explains:
- Delegation sends implementation units to `codex exec` as a structured prompt
- **yolo mode** (`--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox`): Full system access including network. Required for verification steps that run tests or install dependencies. **Recommended.**
- **full-auto mode** (`-s workspace-write`): Workspace-write sandbox, no network access by default. Network can be re-enabled by setting `network_access = true` under `[sandbox_workspace_write]` in `~/.codex/config.toml`.

Present the sandbox mode choice: (1) yolo (recommended), (2) full-auto.

On acceptance:
- Resolve the repo root: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`. Write `work_delegate_consent: true` and `work_delegate_sandbox: <chosen-mode>` to `<repo-root>/.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml`
- To write: (1) if file or directory does not exist, create `<repo-root>/.compound-engineering/` and write the YAML file; (2) if file exists, merge new keys preserving existing keys
- Update `consent_granted` and `sandbox_mode` in the resolved state

On decline:
- Ask whether to disable delegation entirely for this project
- If yes: write `work_delegate: false` to `<repo-root>/.compound-engineering/config.local.yaml` (using the same repo root resolved above). To write: (1) if file or directory does not exist, create `<repo-root>/.compound-engineering/` and write the YAML file; (2) if file exists, merge new keys preserving existing keys. Set `delegation_active` to false, proceed in standard mode
- If no: set `delegation_active` to false for this invocation only, proceed in standard mode

**Headless consent:** If running in a headless or non-interactive context, delegation proceeds only if `work_delegate_consent` is already `true` in the config file. If consent is not recorded, set `delegation_active` to false silently.

## Batching

Delegate all units in one batch. If the plan exceeds 5 units, split into batches at the plan's own phase boundaries, or in groups of roughly 5 -- never splitting units that share files. Skip delegation entirely if every unit is trivial.

## Per-Batch Effort

Each batch picks an effort level proportional to its complexity, then resolves against the config floor before invocation.

**Effort levels — guidelines, not predicates**

Pick the level that best fits the batch. These are signals to weigh, not boxes to tick — use judgment.

- **default (no flag)** — trivial work with no behavioral change: a one-line config tweak, a rename, a typo or comment-only fix, a pure documentation update. Defers to the user's `~/.codex/config.toml` default (which is `medium` on a stock Codex install).
- **`medium`** — small, well-scoped behavioral changes that stay clear of high-risk areas. A handful of files, a single concern, no novel architecture.
- **`high`** — work that touches a high-risk area (auth/session logic, payments, database migrations, external API contracts, error handling with retries/fallbacks), or work spanning enough surface area that one mistake could cascade.
- **`xhigh`** — architectural work: cross-cutting refactors, multiple high-risk areas in the same batch, changes that propagate broadly, or anywhere a wrong call meaningfully degrades the project.

When in doubt, lean up one level — under-resourcing risky work costs more than over-resourcing routine work. Briefly note the picked level and the signal that drove it (e.g., "`high` — touches db/migrations") so the choice is auditable.

A few edge cases worth handling explicitly:
- **Test-only batches:** classify by what the tests *exercise*, not by file paths. Tests for auth flows, payment logic, or migrations get the same level the equivalent implementation work would get.
- **Mixed-complexity batches:** the batch picks one level. If a single batch combines a typo unit and a payments rewrite, pick the higher level. If the spread feels wasteful, prefer splitting at the batching step (see Batching above) over averaging it out.
- **Deletion-only batches:** classify by the risk of what is being removed, not by counts of remaining content. Removing an auth module is `high` even if the batch produces zero `Modify` content.
- **Documentation- or comment-only batches:** `default`.

**Floor and resolution — hard rules**

Effort levels are ordered: `minimal < low < medium < high < xhigh`.

Compute `effective_effort`:

- If `delegate_effort` is unset: `effective_effort = picked_level`.
- If `delegate_effort` is set: substitute `default` → `medium` in `picked_level`, then `effective_effort = max(picked_level, delegate_effort)`.

Emit based on `effective_effort`:

- `medium`, `high`, or `xhigh` → emit `-c 'model_reasoning_effort="<value>"'`.
- `default` → omit the flag (defer to `~/.codex/config.toml`). Reachable only when `delegate_effort` is unset and the pick is `default`.

Never pass the literal string `"default"` to `codex exec`.

Store `effective_effort` as a per-batch derived state value (alongside the session-level `delegate_effort`) and use it in place of `delegate_effort` throughout the Execution Loop.

## Prompt Template

At the start of delegated execution, create a per-run OS-temp scratch directory via `mktemp -d` and capture its **absolute path** for all downstream use. All scratch files for this invocation live under that directory. Do not use `.context/` — these scratch files are per-run throwaway that get cleaned up when delegated execution ends (see Cleanup below), matching the repo Scratch Space convention for one-shot artifacts. Do not pass unresolved shell-variable strings to non-shell tools (Write, Read); use the absolute path returned by `mktemp -d`.

```bash
SCRATCH_DIR="$(mktemp -d -t ce-work-codex-XXXXXX)"
echo "$SCRATCH_DIR"
```

Refer to the echoed absolute path as `<scratch-dir>` throughout the rest of this workflow.

Before each batch, write a prompt file to `<scratch-dir>/prompt-batch-<batch-num>.md`.

Build the prompt from the batch's implementation units using these XML-tagged sections:

```xml
<task>
[For a single-unit batch: Goal from the implementation unit.
For a multi-unit batch: list each unit with its Goal, stating the concrete
job, repository context, and expected end state for each.]
</task>

<files>
[Combined file list from all units in the batch -- files to create, modify, or read.]
</files>

<patterns>
[File paths from all units' "Patterns to follow" fields. If no patterns:
"No explicit patterns referenced -- follow existing conventions in the
modified files."]
</patterns>

<approach>
[For a single-unit batch: Approach from the unit.
For a multi-unit batch: list each unit's approach, noting dependencies
and suggested ordering.]
</approach>

<constraints>
- Do NOT run git commit, git push, or create PRs -- the orchestrating agent handles all git operations
- Restrict all modifications to files within the repository root
- Keep changes tightly scoped to the stated task -- avoid unrelated refactors, renames, or cleanup
- Resolve the task fully before stopping -- do not stop at the first plausible answer
- If you discover mid-execution that you need to modify files outside the repo root, complete what you can within the repo and report what you could not do via the result schema issues field
</constraints>

<testing>
Before writing tests, check whether the plan's test scenarios cover all
categories that apply to each unit. Supplement gaps before writing tests:
- Happy path: core input/output pairs from each unit's goal
- Edge cases: boundary values, empty/nil inputs, type mismatches
- Error/failure paths: invalid inputs, permission denials, downstream failures
- Integration: cross-layer scenarios that mocks alone won't prove

Write tests that name specific inputs and expected outcomes. If your changes
touch code with callbacks, middleware, or event handlers, verify the
interaction chain works end-to-end.
</testing>

<verify>
After implementing, run ALL test files together in a single command (not
per-file). Cross-file contamination (e.g., mocked globals leaking between
test files) only surfaces when tests run in the same process. If tests
fail, fix the issues and re-run until they pass. Do not report status
"completed" unless verification passes. This is your responsibility --
the orchestrator will not re-run verification independently.

[Test and lint commands from the project. Use the union of all units'
verification commands as a single combined invocation.]
</verify>

<output_contract>
Report your result via the --output-schema mechanism. Fill in every field:
- status: "completed" ONLY if all changes were made AND verification passes,
  "partial" if incomplete, "failed" if no meaningful progress
- files_modified: array of file paths you changed
- issues: array of strings describing any problems, gaps, or out-of-scope
  work discovered
- summary: one-paragraph description of what was done
- verification_summary: what you ran to verify (command and outcome).
  Example: "Ran `bun test` -- 14 tests passed, 0 failed."
  If no verification was possible, say why.
</output_contract>
```

## Result Schema

Write the result schema to `<scratch-dir>/result-schema.json` (using the absolute path captured at the start) once at the start of delegated execution:

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "status": { "enum": ["completed", "partial", "failed"] },
    "files_modified": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string" } },
    "issues": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string" } },
    "summary": { "type": "string" },
    "verification_summary": { "type": "string" }
  },
  "required": ["status", "files_modified", "issues", "summary", "verification_summary"],
  "additionalProperties": false
}
```

Each batch's result is written to `<scratch-dir>/result-batch-<batch-num>.json` via the `-o` flag. On plan failure, files are left in place for debugging.

If the result JSON is absent or malformed after a successful exit code, classify as task failure.

## Execution Loop

Initialize a `consecutive_failures` counter at 0 before the first batch.

**Clean-baseline preflight:** Before the first batch, verify there are no uncommitted changes to tracked files:

```bash
git diff --quiet HEAD
```

This intentionally ignores untracked files. Only staged or unstaged modifications to tracked files make rollback unsafe. However, if untracked files exist at paths in the batch's planned Files list, rollback (`git clean -fd -- <paths>`) would delete them. If such overlaps are detected, warn the user and recommend committing or stashing those files before proceeding.

If tracked files are dirty, stop and present options: (1) commit current changes, (2) stash explicitly (`git stash push -m "pre-delegation"`), (3) continue in standard mode (sets `delegation_active` to false). Do not auto-stash user changes.

**Delegation invocation:** For each batch, execute these as **separate Bash tool calls** (not combined into one):

**Step A — Launch (background, separate Bash call):**

Write the prompt file, then make a single Bash tool call with `run_in_background: true` set on the tool parameter. This call returns immediately and has no timeout ceiling.

Substitute the literal absolute path captured at setup for every `<scratch-dir>` below. Each Bash tool call starts a fresh shell, so the `$SCRATCH_DIR` variable from the setup snippet is not preserved — an unresolved `$SCRATCH_DIR` would expand empty and break result detection.

```bash
# Substitute the resolved sandbox_mode value (yolo or full-auto) from the skill state
SANDBOX_MODE="<sandbox_mode>"

# Resolve sandbox flag
if [ "$SANDBOX_MODE" = "full-auto" ]; then
  SANDBOX_FLAG="-s workspace-write"
else
  SANDBOX_FLAG="--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox"
fi

codex exec \
  $SANDBOX_FLAG \
  --output-schema "<scratch-dir>/result-schema.json" \
  -o "<scratch-dir>/result-batch-<batch-num>.json" \
  - < "<scratch-dir>/prompt-batch-<batch-num>.md"
```

**Conditional flags** — only include each line when the corresponding skill-state value is set:

- If `delegate_model` is set, insert `  -m "<delegate_model>" \` as a line before `$SANDBOX_FLAG`.
- If `effective_effort` is `medium`, `high`, or `xhigh` (resolved via Per-Batch Effort above), insert `  -c 'model_reasoning_effort="<effective_effort>"' \` as a line before `$SANDBOX_FLAG`. When `effective_effort` is `default` (only possible when `delegate_effort` is unset and the pick is `default`), omit the line — never pass the literal string `"default"`.

When either value is unset, omit its line entirely — Codex resolves the default from the user's `~/.codex/config.toml` (and ultimately the CLI's own built-in default). Do not substitute a placeholder string for unset values.

Critical: `run_in_background: true` must be set as a **Bash tool parameter**, not as a shell `&` suffix. The tool parameter is what removes the timeout ceiling. A shell `&` inside a foreground Bash call still hits the 2-minute default timeout.

Quoting is critical for the `-c` flag when present: use single quotes around the entire key=value and double quotes around the TOML string value inside. Example: `-c 'model_reasoning_effort="high"'`.

Do not improvise CLI flags or modify this invocation template beyond the documented conditional insertions.

**Step B — Poll (foreground, separate Bash calls):**

After the launch call returns, make a **new, separate** foreground Bash tool call that polls for the result file. This keeps the agent's turn active so the user cannot interfere with the working tree.

Substitute the literal absolute path captured at setup for `<scratch-dir>`. The shell variable from Step A does not survive across separate Bash tool calls.

```bash
RESULT_FILE="<scratch-dir>/result-batch-<batch-num>.json"
for i in $(seq 1 6); do
  test -s "$RESULT_FILE" && echo "DONE" && exit 0
  sleep 10
done
echo "Waiting for Codex..."
```

If the output is "Waiting for Codex...", issue the same polling command again as another separate Bash call. Repeat until the output is "DONE", then read the result file and proceed to classification.

**Polling termination conditions:** Stop polling when any of these conditions is met:

- **Result file appears** (output is "DONE") -- proceed to result classification normally.
- **Background process exits with non-zero code** -- classify as CLI failure (row 1). Rollback and fall back to standard mode.
- **Background process exits with zero code but result file is absent** -- classify as task failure (row 2: exit 0, result JSON missing). Rollback and increment `consecutive_failures`.
- **5 polling rounds** elapse (~5 minutes) without the result file appearing and without a background process notification -- treat as a hung process. Classify as CLI failure (row 1). Rollback and fall back to standard mode.

**Result classification:** Codex is responsible for running verification internally and fixing failures before reporting -- the orchestrator does not re-run verification independently.

| # | Signal | Classification | Action |
|---|--------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | Exit code != 0 | CLI failure | Rollback to HEAD. Fall back to standard mode for ALL remaining work. |
| 2 | Exit code 0, result JSON missing or malformed | Task failure | Rollback to HEAD. Increment `consecutive_failures`. |
| 3 | Exit code 0, `status: "failed"` | Task failure | Rollback to HEAD. Increment `consecutive_failures`. |
| 4 | Exit code 0, `status: "partial"` | Partial success | Keep the diff. Complete remaining work locally, verify, and commit. Increment `consecutive_failures`. |
| 5 | Exit code 0, `status: "completed"` | Success | Commit changes. Reset `consecutive_failures` to 0. |

**Result handoff — surface to user:** After reading the result JSON and before committing or rolling back, display a summary so the user sees what happened. Format:

> **Codex batch <batch-num> — <classification>**
> <summary from result JSON>
>
> **Files:** <comma-separated list from files_modified>
> **Verification:** <verification_summary from result JSON>
> **Issues:** <issues list, or "None">

On failure or partial results, include the classification reason (e.g., "status: failed", "result JSON missing") so the user understands why the orchestrator is rolling back or completing locally.

Keep this brief — the goal is transparency, not a wall of text. One short block per batch.

**Rollback procedure:**

```bash
git checkout -- .
git clean -fd -- <paths from the batch's combined Files list>
```

Do NOT use bare `git clean -fd` without path arguments.

**Commit on success:**

```bash
git add $(git diff --name-only HEAD; git ls-files --others --exclude-standard)
git commit -m "feat(<scope>): <batch summary>"
```

**Between batches** (plans split into multiple batches): Report what completed, test results, and what's next. Continue immediately unless the user intervenes -- the checkpoint exists so the user *can* steer, not so they *must*.

**Circuit breaker:** After 3 consecutive failures, set `delegation_active` to false and emit: "Codex delegation disabled after 3 consecutive failures -- completing remaining units in standard mode."

**Scratch cleanup:** No explicit cleanup needed — OS temp handles eventual cleanup (macOS `$TMPDIR` periodic purge; Linux/WSL `/tmp` reboot or periodic cleanup). Leaving `<scratch-dir>` in place after the run also preserves intermediate artifacts for debugging if anything went wrong.

## Mixed-Model Attribution

When some units are executed by Codex and others locally:
- If all units used delegation: attribute to the Codex model
- If all units used standard mode: attribute to the current agent's model
- If mixed: note which units were delegated in the PR description and credit both models

Three INDEPENDENT units (no shared files). Implement all three:

U1 — Manifest schema `canonical.auto` + loader/spec reconciliation.
  Goal: Add an opt-out field so `sync` auto-advance can be disabled per manifest, and reconcile the loader so an advancing manifest is valid.

U2 — Materialize resolve-or-fallback mode.
  Goal: Add a materialize path that resolves the newest upstream commit online and, on network failure, falls back to the cached pin with a warning (never errors).

U6 — `--frozen` flag on `sync`.
  Goal: Add the per-invocation opt-out flag to the sync command surface. (It is consumed later by U5, which is NOT in this batch — for now just add and register the flag and thread its value to where the prepare-path decision will read it; do not implement auto-advance behavior itself.)
</task>

<files>
U1: internal/manifest/schema.go, internal/manifest/load.go, docs/spec/manifest-v1.md, internal/manifest/load_test.go, internal/manifest/schema_test.go (create if absent)

U6: internal/cli/cmd_sync.go, internal/cli/cmd_sync_test.go
Read for context: internal/manifest/write.go, internal/cli/cmd_update.go, internal/cli/setup.go, internal/git/shell.go
</files>

<patterns>
U1: mirror existing `omitempty` fields in CanonicalSource; follow the additive-schema PR checklist in AGENTS.md. Strict load uses yaml.DisallowUnknownField() (internal/manifest/load.go ~line 113) — a new field MUST be added to the struct.
U2: mirror the existing zero-network fast path in internal/git/materialize.go (~lines 123-137), ErrRemoteUnreachable handling in internal/git/shell.go, and the input-shape guards (~lines 102-118).
U6: mirror existing BoolVar flags declared/registered in newSyncCommand (internal/cli/cmd_sync.go ~line 32 declare, ~line 138 register).
</patterns>

<approach>
U1:
- Add `Auto *bool `yaml:"auto,omitempty"`` to CanonicalSource in schema.go. Semantics: nil ⇒ auto-on (default); explicit false ⇒ opt out. Use *bool because plain bool+omitempty cannot express default-true.
- In load.go, verify the new field passes strict load, and that old manifests without `auto` still load. Confirm the existing invariants still hold for an auto manifest: (a) `canonical.commit` MUST equal `trusted_sha` when both set; (b) the "trusted_sha set but canonical.commit empty" hybrid remains rejected. An auto manifest always re-pins to a concrete SHA each run, so both keys stay present and equal — do NOT loosen these invariants; just confirm an auto manifest satisfies them.
- Update docs/spec/manifest-v1.md: replace/augment the `floating` "reserved; not yet in v1 schema" section to document `canonical.auto` instead, and update the validation table to include `auto`.

U2:
- Add a new input mode to the Input struct (e.g. `ResolveOrFallback bool`), mutually exclusive with `Floating` and with pinned-strict offline. Do NOT relax the existing `Floating && Offline` hard-refuse — this is a separate, new mode.
- Behavior: when ResolveOrFallback is set and a PinnedSHA is present: attempt online ref resolution (ResolveRef against CanonicalURL/Ref). On `git.ErrRemoteUnreachable`, return a Result using the cached pin (FromCache true) and signal that a fallback/warning occurred, without erroring. On success, return the newly resolved SHA. If unreachable AND no cache present, return a clear error.
- Preserve the existing reachability defense (IsAncestor force-push guard) on the resolved SHA.
- Add input-shape guards rejecting ResolveOrFallback combined with Floating.

U6:
- Declare `frozen bool` alongside the other sync flag vars; register with cmd.Flags().BoolVar with a clear help string ("Disable auto-advance; sync at the pinned commit only"). Thread the value into the options/struct the prepare path will consult. Compose cleanly with existing flags (--best-effort, --expect-deletions, --post-merge, --target, --user). Do not implement advance logic.

Ordering: U1, U2, U6 are independent — any order.
</approach>

- U1 happy: manifest with `auto: false` loads (Auto non-nil false); manifest without auto loads (Auto nil); `auto: true` round-trips. Edge/error: old pre-auto manifest still validates; commit==trusted_sha both present passes; empty-commit-with-trusted_sha hybrid still rejected; unknown sibling field under canonical still rejected.
- U2 happy: online + newer ref resolves to new SHA. - U6 happy: `sync --frozen` parses and sets the flag; help text present. Edge: --frozen composes with --best-effort and --post-merge without conflict.

content.text: The new auto-advance posture sits inside the `ManifestTrustedSHA` branch. The load-bearing security property is that the revoked-anchor check runs *before* this and returns early — otherwise auto could override a revocation. The diff doesn't show that ordering. Let me read the full `Decide` function to verify.

input.command: sed -n '95,175p' /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/trust/policy.go
input.description: Read full Decide function to verify revoke precedence

content.text:
The security ordering is correct: **step 1 "Revoke beats everything" returns `KindRevokedBlock` before the pin branch**, so a revoked anchor always refuses even under `PostureAllowNewSHAs`. The auto-advance path requires all four conditions (pin present, drift, posture on, `FastForward` proven) — and a non-ff (rewritten history) falls through to the fail-closed path. The `FastForward` flag is caller-supplied proof, keeping the policy engine git-free. This is a clean, correct design.

Let me verify the U3 extraction preserved `update`'s behavior and check the new trust types.

input.command: echo "=========== trust/types.go ==========="; git --no-pager diff HEAD -- internal/trust/types.go; echo; echo "=========== cli/advance.go (new) ==========="; sed -n '1,120p' /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/cli/advance.go
input.description: Review trust types and new advance.go helper

content.text:
The U3 extraction is clean and faithful — `resolveAdvance` computes ff via `IsAncestor` (unreadable ancestry → ff=false, matching `update`'s prior behavior), and `repinManifest` reuses `WriteResolvedSHA`. New trust types (`KindProceedAutoAdvance`, `Posture`) are well-documented.

**One important integration seam for U5:** `resolveAdvanceTarget` resolves URLs via `Materialize{Floating: true}` — which requires network. That's correct for `update` (which refuses offline anyway), but sync's **offline-fallback rail (R4)** needs the `ResolveOrFallback` mode from U2. So U5 can't call `resolveAdvance` verbatim for the offline path — it'll need the materialize mode parameterized or offline handled before the call. Noting this for the B3 design.

Let me confirm `update`'s behavior is preserved (that 1-line test change) and then commit B2.

content.text: **Pre-B3 gate.** B3 (U5) is the unit that flips auto-advance on by default — the highest-blast-radius change. Before delegating it I committed to two things: an independent code review of the trust/materialize core, and resolving the hierarchy run-lock design decision. input.prompt:
Adversarially review a security-critical change on the current branch of the Go repo agent-sync at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync. The branch is `feat/inline-auto-update`; review the diff of the last two commits against the branch point (`git diff main...HEAD` or `git log --oneline main..HEAD` to orient, then inspect the commits d63b96e and 53b5412).

Context: this feature makes `agent-sync sync` auto-advance the canonical git pin to newest upstream by default, auto-accepting the trust decision THROUGH the trust policy engine (not bypassing it). Safety rails: fast-forward-only advancement, offline falls back to cached pin, revoked trust anchors must ALWAYS refuse. The trust gate (`trusted_sha`) is agent-sync's only supply-chain defense — there is no signature verification.

Your job is to try to BREAK the security guarantees. Focus on these files/areas:
- internal/trust/policy.go — the `Decide` function, new `PostureAllowNewSHAs` / `KindProceedAutoAdvance`. 
- internal/trust/types.go — new Posture/Kind types.
- internal/git/materialize.go — new `ResolveOrFallback` mode and `fallbackToPinned`.
- internal/cli/advance.go — `resolveAdvance` (ff computation via git.IsAncestor), `repinManifest`.
- internal/manifest/schema.go, load.go — the `canonical.auto *bool` field.

Specifically hunt for:
1. Can auto-advance EVER proceed across a revoked trust anchor? Trace every path in Decide. Confirm revoke is checked before the PostureAllowNewSHAs branch and there is no path that reaches KindProceedAutoAdvance with State.Revoked true.
2. Can auto-advance proceed across REWRITTEN/force-pushed history (non-fast-forward)? The auto path requires FastForward==true. Verify FastForward is genuinely computed from git.IsAncestor(oldPin, newSHA) and that an error in IsAncestor is treated as non-ff (fail-safe), not ff. Look for any way FastForward could be true without a real ancestry check.
3. In materialize.go ResolveOrFallback / fallbackToPinned: can it fall back to a pin that isn't actually cached, or return a stale/wrong SHA silently? Can the reachability (IsAncestor) check be skipped? Can offline fallback mask a real force-push?
4. Does the `canonical.auto *bool` handling have a nil-deref or a default-inversion bug (nil should mean auto-ON per spec)? Does an `auto:false` manifest actually disable the auto path everywhere it's read?
5. Any TOCTOU or atomicity gaps between resolve, ff-check, re-pin (repinManifest via WriteResolvedSHA+WriteFile), and materialize?
6. Does the change weaken any EXISTING guarantee — e.g. does `update`'s behavior actually stay identical, does CI still fail closed for non-auto pin mismatch?

Also check AGENTS.md invariants: all fs writes via internal/fsroot; non-interactive paths never prompt/hang; additive schema changes.

Report concrete findings ranked by severity with file:line references and a specific failure scenario for each (inputs → wrong outcome). If you find nothing exploitable, say so explicitly and note what you verified. Do NOT modify files — read-only review.

input.description: Read locking code in update, hierarchy sync, engine

content.text:
I've resolved the run-lock design decision (KTD8) by reading the locking model:

- `engine.Sync` self-acquires the per-workspace `RunLock` unless `RunLockHeld=true` (re-acquiring would deadlock). `update` already solves atomic re-pin+sync: acquire the scope's `RunLock` at the CLI level → re-pin → `engine.Sync(RunLockHeld=true)`.
- **U5 decision:** apply that same discipline *per scope*, but only for scopes eligible to auto-advance (url/local_path, not `--frozen`, `auto != false`). Non-eligible scopes (frozen, `auto:false`, `local_dir`) keep today's path where `engine.Sync` self-locks — zero blast radius for them. The re-pin must happen **before** `prepareScope` so prepare materializes the new pin.

I also pinned down the offline seam I flagged earlier: `resolveAdvance` uses `Floating` (online-required), so for URL sources on the sync path U5 must resolve via the `ResolveOrFallback` materialize mode instead — `FellBackToPinned` → warn + sync at existing pin, no advance; otherwise ff-check → `trust.Decide` → re-pin on `KindProceedAutoAdvance`, or report-and-continue on refusal.

That's the full U5 design. I'm **holding B3 until the adversarial trust review comes back** — per my commitment, I won't build the activation on the trust core until it's cleared. The review is running now; I'll fold any findings into the U5 prompt (or fix B2 first) when it lands.

Current state:
- B1 `d63b96e` ✅ · B2 `53b5412` ✅ (both independently `-race` verified)
- Adversarial trust review: 🔄 running
- B3 (U5+U7): design ready, gated on the review
- B4 (U8 docs): queued

I'll report back when the review lands.

**Verified safe (no break found):**
- Revoke ordering in `Decide` is correct — the `State.Revoked` early-return (policy.go:110) precedes the `PostureAllowNewSHAs` branch (policy.go:121); no path reaches `KindProceedAutoAdvance` with `Revoked==true` *given State is populated*.
- `resolveAdvance` computes ff genuinely from `git.IsAncestor(oldPin, newSHA)` and treats an `IsAncestor` error as `fastForward=false` (advance.go:36-40) — fail-safe, identical to the pre-refactor update code.
- `fallbackToPinned` (materialize.go:263-277) returns the cached pin only after `cacheHasSha` confirms presence; otherwise hard-errors. It never returns a resolved/force-pushed head, so offline fallback cannot adopt a rewritten history — it can only stay at the trusted pin.
- Online `ResolveOrFallback` runs the `IsAncestor(pin, target)` reachability check unconditionally (materialize.go:225-232, guarded by input validation requiring both PinnedSHA and Ref) — the check cannot be skipped, and an error is a hard fail.
- Existing guarantees intact: `materializeURL` still passes `PostureDefault` (zero), so CI non-auto pin-mismatch still fails closed (policy.go:137). The `update` refactor is a faithful extraction (behavior byte-identical). `manifest.WriteFile` (write.go:112-118) still routes through `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot` + `StagedWrite`, honoring the AGENTS.md fs invariant. The `Auto *bool` schema addition is additive.

content.text:
The adversarial review is high-value. Verdict: nothing is exploitable *today* (the primitives are unwired), but it found **two concrete traps the U5 wiring must not fall into**, plus a structural weakness worth fixing now:

1. **HIGH — revoke defense is caller-contingent.** `Decide`'s revoke check only fires if the caller populated `State` via `Store.Fold()`. The one existing caller passes a zero `State`. If U5 copies that pattern and adds the auto posture, a **revoked anchor would be silently advanced past** — defeating the kill-switch. The reviewer's recommendation: make it *structural*, not caller-dependent.
2. **MEDIUM — ff proof baseline divergence.** `resolveAdvance` proves fast-forward against `canonical.commit`, but `Decide` gates the anchor on `trusted_sha`. If they differ (or commit is empty → ff defaults to `true` with *no* check), the ff rail is bypassed for the auto path.
3. **LOW — `--frozen`/`auto:false` unread** (expected; U5 consumes them — but must land with tests).

I'm going to **harden the trust layer myself right now** — the highest-severity finding deserves a structural fail-closed guard, and it's a surgical change to code I've already reviewed closely. Then I'll bake findings 2 & 3 and the testing gaps into the U5 prompt. Let me read the exact `Decide` input and the existing caller.

<task>
You are implementing Batch 3 of the plan at `docs/plans/2026-07-08-001-feat-inline-auto-update-plan.md` in the Go repo `agent-sync` (Go 1.25). READ THE PLAN FIRST (esp. U5, U7, KTD1/KTD4/KTD8), plus `AGENTS.md` (invariants: all fs writes via internal/fsroot; non-interactive paths NEVER prompt/hang; per-scope continue-and-report). This is the ACTIVATION batch — it turns auto-advance ON by default. It is security-critical.

Batches 1 & 2 already landed and are on this branch:
- manifest `CanonicalSource.Auto *bool` (nil ⇒ auto-ON; false ⇒ opt out).
- `internal/git/materialize.go` `ResolveOrFallback` Input mode → resolves ref online, falls back to cached pin on ErrRemoteUnreachable, returns `Result.FellBackToPinned`; enforces ff via IsAncestor.
- `internal/cli/advance.go`: `resolveAdvance` (resolves newest + computes `fastForward` via git.IsAncestor against `m.Canonical.Commit`), `repinManifest` (WriteResolvedSHA+WriteFile).
- `internal/trust`: `Decide` with `Posture` + `KindProceedAutoAdvance`. IMPORTANT: `Decide` now REQUIRES `DecideInput.StateLoaded = true` when `Posture == PostureAllowNewSHAs`, else it fails closed with ErrTrustDecisionRequired. `StateLoaded` must only be set true after you actually load trust State from the store (trust.Store.Fold), so real revocations are detected.
- `internal/cli/cmd_sync.go`: `--frozen` flag currently stashed in context via `withSyncRequest`/`syncRequestFrom` (a bridge — see U5 task 3).

Two units, U5 then U7:

U5 — Wire auto-advance into the per-scope sync prepare path.
U7 — Record every auto-advance (old→new SHA) to the structured log + the cache audit file.
</task>

<files>
U5: internal/cli/setup.go (prepareScope), internal/cli/cmd_sync.go (single-scope path + --frozen threading), internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go (per-scope loop, continue-and-report for refused advance), internal/cli/advance.go (add an auto-path resolve + ff-baseline fix), internal/cli/materialize.go (use ResolveOrFallback on the auto path), internal/cli/cmd_sync_test.go, internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go, internal/cli/advance_test.go
U7: the advance helper / setup.go (log emit), internal/cache (audit write — see cache.WriteAudit referenced in internal/git/materialize.go), plus tests
Read for context: internal/cli/cmd_update.go (run-lock discipline: NewRunLock/Acquire + syncAfterRepin with RunLockHeld=true), internal/engine/engine.go (Options.RunLockHeld ~line 147-167), internal/locks/runlock.go, internal/trust/store.go (Fold), internal/trust/policy.go (DecideInput), internal/git/materialize.go.
</files>

<patterns>
- Run-lock: mirror cmd_update.go exactly — open fsroot workspace root, NewRunLock(root), Acquire with a timeout, defer release, re-pin, then engine.Sync with req.Options.RunLockHeld=true (re-acquiring inside engine would deadlock; see engine.go comment).
- Continue-and-report: mirror hierarchy_sync.go scopeOutcome — a refused/failed advance for one scope sets out.Err (or a warning) and the loop continues; it must NOT abort the whole run.
- Offline best-effort: mirror composeUserRules' handling of ErrRemoteUnreachable — warn and proceed with the cached pin.
- Trust load: mirror how the trust Store is opened/folded elsewhere in internal/cli (find the existing Store usage) to populate State before Decide.
</patterns>

<approach>
Per-scope decision, placed where the loaded manifest is in hand (in or adjacent to prepareScope, and in the single-scope path). For EACH scope:

1. Eligibility: auto-advance applies only when the source is git-backed (Canonical.URL or Canonical.LocalPath) AND NOT `--frozen` AND `Canonical.Auto` is not explicitly false (nil ⇒ ON). `Canonical.LocalDir` sources are a NO-OP (no pin to advance). Frozen / auto:false / local_dir ⇒ today's behavior unchanged (do NOT acquire the CLI run lock for these; let engine.Sync self-lock as it does now).

2. For an eligible scope, acquire the scope's CLI-level RunLock (like update) and hold it across re-pin + prepare + sync. Then:
   a. Resolve newest with OFFLINE FALLBACK. For URL sources, use git.Materialize with `ResolveOrFallback: true`, `PinnedSHA: m.Canonical.Commit`, `Ref: m.Canonical.Ref`. If `Result.FellBackToPinned` (remote unreachable) ⇒ WARN, do NOT advance, proceed to prepare+sync at the existing pin. For LocalPath sources, resolve the ref locally (offline is not a concern).
   b. FAST-FORWARD BASELINE FIX (review finding #2): compute the ff proof against the SAME SHA the trust engine gates on — `m.TrustedSHA` (ManifestTrustedSHA) — NOT `m.Canonical.Commit`. If the baseline (trusted_sha) is empty, treat fastForward as FALSE (fail-safe), never default-true. Add/adjust an auto-path resolve in advance.go so the ff baseline is trusted_sha for the auto path. (Leave update's existing resolveAdvance using Canonical.Commit unchanged — update writes them equal and has a human gate.)
   c. Load trust State: open the trust Store and Fold for the canonical URL so revocations are seen. Call trust.Decide with Posture=PostureAllowNewSHAs, FastForward=<the ff verdict from (b)>, ManifestTrustedSHA=m.TrustedSHA, StateLoaded=true, and the resolved SHA.
   d. On `KindProceedAutoAdvance`: append the returned AppendPending to pending.jsonl (reuse the existing writer), repinManifest(newSHA), then continue to prepare+sync (which now materializes the new pin). On a refusal (KindDecisionRequired from non-ff, or ErrRevokedTrustAnchor, or any error): do NOT advance, report the scope (continue-and-report), keep the existing pin, and — for revoke — surface the revoke error clearly. NEVER prompt/hang in non-interactive mode.
   e. Re-pin MUST happen BEFORE prepareScope so prepare reads the advanced manifest and materializes the new SHA.
   f. If re-pin SUCCEEDS but the subsequent engine.Sync FAILS: surface a distinct condition/exit signal meaning "pin moved but files did not land — re-run sync" (model on cmd_update.go's exitUpdatePinMoved = 6). Do not roll back the pin silently.

Adversarially review the NOW-WIRED auto-advance feature on the `feat/inline-auto-update` branch of the Go repo agent-sync at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync. The activation was just implemented. Inspect the uncommitted working-tree diff (`git diff HEAD`) plus the committed context (`git log --oneline main..HEAD`). Focus on the sync auto-advance wiring in internal/cli/setup.go (prepareScopeForSync, prepareAutoAdvanceScope, shouldAutoAdvanceSync), internal/cli/advance.go (resolveAutoAdvance, resolveRemoteAutoAdvanceTarget), internal/cli/cmd_sync.go, internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go, internal/cli/materialize.go, internal/trust/policy.go.

Background: `agent-sync sync` now auto-advances the canonical git pin to newest upstream by DEFAULT, auto-accepting the trust decision THROUGH the trust policy engine. Security rails that MUST hold: (a) a revoked trust anchor NEVER auto-advances; (b) fast-forward-only — never advance across rewritten/force-pushed history; (c) `--frozen` and `canonical.auto:false` each force pinned-only, no advance; (d) offline/unreachable falls back to the cached pin, never errors; (e) non-interactive paths NEVER prompt or hang. The trust gate (trusted_sha) is the ONLY supply-chain defense — there is no signature verification. Note: trust/revoke is keyed by canonical URL; local_path sources are intentionally not trust-gated (consistent with existing materializeLocal behavior), but ff-only must still apply to them.

Try to BREAK these guarantees. Specifically hunt for:
1. Any path where sync auto-advances across a REVOKED anchor. Verify prepareAutoAdvanceScope calls loadTrustState (Store.Fold) and passes StateLoaded=true, and that Decide's revoke check precedes the posture branch. Check the URL branch specifically.
2. Any path where a NON-fast-forward (rewritten history) advance proceeds. Verify resolveAutoAdvance computes ff against m.TrustedSHA (NOT canonical.commit), that empty trusted_sha ⇒ fastForward=false, and that an IsAncestor error ⇒ false. Check both URL and local_path branches.
3. Can `--frozen` or `canonical.auto:false` be bypassed so an advance happens anyway? Trace shouldAutoAdvanceSync and its callers (single-scope runSingleScopeSync and hierarchy runHierarchySync).
4. Offline/unreachable: can the fallback adopt a wrong/force-pushed SHA instead of the cached pin? Can it error the whole sync instead of falling back? Check resolveRemoteAutoAdvanceTarget's two-Materialize composition.
5. TOCTOU / atomicity: is the run lock held across resolve→ff-check→Fold→Decide→repin→materialize→emit? Can a concurrent run or a remote move between ff-proof and emit cause a wrong SHA to land? Note the ResolvedGit bypass decodes the exact approved SHA — verify that closes the window.
6. Hierarchy: does a refused/revoked scope abort the whole run (bad) or continue-and-report (good)? Can one scope's advance corrupt another's? Are per-scope run locks correct?
7. The pin-moved-but-emit-failed path (exitUpdatePinMoved) and the audit-write-failure path: can they leave the manifest re-pinned but the workspace inconsistent in a way that a re-run does NOT fix?
8. local_path auto-advance: ff-only must still apply even though trust.Decide is skipped. Verify.
9. Does the change break any EXISTING guarantee — update's behavior, the pinned-path CI fail-closed, validate/watch (which use prepareScope, not prepareScopeForSync)?

Also verify AGENTS.md invariants: all fs writes via internal/fsroot; non-interactive never hangs.

Report concrete findings ranked by severity with file:line refs and a specific failure scenario (inputs → wrong outcome) for each. If a guarantee holds, state what you verified. Do NOT modify files — read-only.

      "confidence": 60,

      "autofix_class": "advisory",

      "evidence": [
        "prepareScopeForSync loads the manifest at setup.go:111 BEFORE any lock exists, then hands that same *m into prepareAutoAdvanceScope.",
        "prepareAutoAdvanceScope acquires the per-workspace run lock at setup.go:281, then calls resolveAutoAdvance(ctx, m) at setup.go:289 using the PRE-LOCK m. advance.go:61 sets baseline := m.TrustedSHA (pre-lock) and proves fast-forward against it; trust.Decide at setup.go:313 is passed ManifestTrustedSHA: m.TrustedSHA (pre-lock) and its AppendPending.OldSHA is that pre-lock value. The manifest is only reloaded AFTER repin (setup.go ~356), never before the ff-proof.",
        "Interleaving: Process B holds the run lock and advances the manifest X->Y (repins trusted_sha=Y, commit=Y), releases. Process A loaded m with trusted_sha=X before B wrote, then blocks on Acquire (2s timeout), acquires after B, and computes ff and Decide against stale X. Decide compares resolved Z against stale X (not on-disk Y).",
        "Consequence 1 (mechanically certain given the interleave): the auto-advance audit record and pending-trust entry record OldSHA = X, but the pin actually moved from Y; the security audit trail is wrong.",
        "Consequence 2 (needs a force-push interleave): if the remote is rewritten after B such that Z descends from X but NOT from Y, A proves ff against X and lands Z via KindProceedAutoAdvance — a non-fast-forward advance relative to the actual on-disk trusted anchor Y, defeating guarantee (b).",
        "The run lock is intended to serialize exactly these same-workspace runs, but because the manifest read that feeds the ff baseline happens outside the lock, serialization is incomplete. Correct shape: reload the manifest under the lock and recompute baseline/Decide from the fresh trusted_sha."
      ]
    },
    {
      "title": "State divergence: with empty trusted_sha (or an auto-promote decision) sync materializes the floating-newest SHA instead of canonical.commit, without repinning or auditing, and silently drops the promote trust-log record",
      "severity": "medium",

      "autofix_class": "advisory",
      "owner": "human",
      "evidence": [

        "In prepareAutoAdvanceScope's URL branch, out.ResolvedGit.Commit is set to res.newSHA (the newest resolved SHA), not canonical.commit.",
        "When m.TrustedSHA == \"\", trust.Decide skips its step-2 manifest-pin/ff gate entirely and falls to step 3 (user history). If the user's trust log has CurrentSHA == res.newSHA, Decide returns KindProceed; if AllowNewSHAs+cooldown, it returns KindProceedAutoPromote with an AppendTrustLog record.",
        "Both kinds hit the `dec.Kind != trust.KindProceedAutoAdvance` branch at setup.go:334, which does `return out, nil` — no repinManifest, no AppendAutoAdvance audit, and dec.AppendTrustLog is never written. The subsequent materialize uses ResolvedGit.Commit = res.newSHA.",
        "Result: the workspace is emitted at the floating-newest SHA (which differs from canonical.commit), while the manifest pin stays stale, no auto-advance audit line is written, and for the AutoPromote case the trust-log promote record is lost (so State.CurrentSHA never updates and the same silent divergence repeats every run). The emitted SHA is trust-approved so it is not a supply-chain hole, but manifest pin, emitted content, and audit trail are inconsistent and the ff-only gate does not run on that SHA."
      ]
    },
    {
      "title": "Latent revoke-gate bypass: trust routing keys on canonical.URL while target resolution keys on canonical.LocalPath — safe only because manifest validation enforces exactly-one canonical source",

        "If a manifest ever had BOTH url and local_path set, resolveAutoAdvanceTarget would resolve the local path while prepareAutoAdvanceScope would take the URL branch and call loadTrustState(res.sourceURL) with res.sourceURL == the LOCAL PATH string — which never matches a revoke keyed by the canonical URL, so a revoked URL anchor would auto-advance.",
        "Currently defended: manifest/load.go:156 enforces `exactly one of url, local_path, or local_dir`, so both-set is rejected at LoadFile and this path is unreachable. This is a latent coupling — the revoke guarantee here depends on the one-of invariant staying intact rather than on the two branches agreeing on the source. A defensive fix is to derive both the routing decision and the source from the same predicate."
      ]
    },
    {
      "title": "Offline auto-advance fallback errors the whole sync when the cached pin is absent, rather than degrading to pinned-only",
      "severity": "low",
      "confidence": 85,
      "location": "internal/cli/advance.go resolveRemoteAutoAdvanceTarget (two-Materialize composition; fallback via git.Materialize PinnedSHA+Offline)",
      "autofix_class": "advisory",
      "owner": "human",
      "evidence": [
        "resolveRemoteAutoAdvanceTarget first does Materialize{Floating:true}; on git.ErrRemoteUnreachable it does Materialize{PinnedSHA: m.Canonical.Commit, Offline:true}. The fallback correctly targets the cached pin (never a force-pushed floating SHA), so it cannot adopt a wrong SHA — guarantee (d)'s 'wrong SHA' half holds.",
        "But git.Materialize offline fast-path (materialize.go:143-155) returns ErrUnresolvablePinOffline when the pinned commit is not present in the cache. resolveRemoteAutoAdvanceTarget then returns `resolve remote ref: ...; cached pin fallback failed: ...`, which propagates up and errors the entire sync.",
        "Trigger: remote transiently unreachable AND canonical.commit not in cache (first-ever sync attempted while the network is down, or the cache dir was pruned/cleared). Guarantee (d)'s 'never errors, falls back to the cached pin' is violated for this state. It is genuinely unrecoverable offline and matches pre-existing pinned-sync behavior, so severity is low — but it is worth an explicit test and a clearer message distinguishing 'nothing cached yet' from a transient failure."
      ]

The emit SHA window is closed by the ResolvedGit bypass (materializeResolvedGit decodes the exact approved res.newSHA from the mirror), but concurrent AppendAutoAdvance writes to the shared audit are only staged-atomic per-write, not serialized, so interleaved advances from two workspaces could interleave/last-writer-win audit lines. Hierarchy is continue-and-report (per-scope closure sets out.Err and the loop proceeds; per-scope run locks per root). pin-moved / audit-write-failure paths leave the manifest repinned (trusted_sha==commit==newSHA), so a re-run yields Decide KindProceed and re-materializes — recoverable. prepareScope (validate/watch) is unchanged: still materializes with Offline and buildPrepared(runLockHeld=false, pinMovedTo=\"\")."

content.text:
The re-review is high-value and found **real issues** — I'm not going to commit B3 until the material ones are fixed. Evaluating each critically:

- **Finding 1 (HIGH) — TOCTOU on the ff baseline.** Legitimate and important. The manifest is loaded *before* the run lock, so the ff-proof and `Decide` run against a pre-lock `trusted_sha`. Under a concurrent same-workspace advance, the audit records the wrong `OldSHA`, and with a force-push interleave it can defeat ff-only. **Must fix:** reload the manifest *under* the lock and recompute from the fresh anchor.
- **Finding 2 (MEDIUM) — empty `trusted_sha` divergence.** Legitimate. `shouldAutoAdvanceSync` fires on `commit != ""` without requiring `trusted_sha`, so an empty-anchor manifest can materialize the floating-newest SHA with no repin/audit and drop the promote log. **Must fix:** require `trusted_sha` for the auto path (empty-anchor → normal pinned/TOFU path), and materialize the existing pin on any non-advance.
- **Finding 3 (LOW) — routing predicate coupling.** Real but latent (blocked by the one-of manifest invariant). Cheap defensive fix — I'll align the predicates.
- **Finding 4 (LOW) — offline with no cache errors.** I agree with the reviewer's own note: this is acceptable — you can't fall back to a pin that was never cached, and it matches today's pinned-offline behavior. I'll document it with a test rather than change behavior.

Fixing 1–3 myself (surgical, security-critical — I want tight control). Let me read the exact region.

All three are legitimate; Finding 4 I'm accepting as-is (documented behavior). Let me reason through the cleanest fixes:

- **Fix 1:** reload the manifest under the lock and drive `resolveAutoAdvance`/`Decide`/repin from the fresh `trusted_sha`.
- **Fix 2:** require `trusted_sha != ""` in `shouldAutoAdvanceSync`. This is complete, not partial — with a non-empty anchor, `Decide`'s step-2 (pin authoritative) always returns within that block and never falls through to the auto-promote/user-history path, and `res.newSHA == commit` in every non-advance case, so the floating-newest divergence becomes unreachable.
- **Fix 3:** gate the trust call on `m.Canonical.LocalPath == ""` (identical to today under the one-of invariant, but agrees with `resolveAutoAdvanceTarget` even in the hypothetical both-set case → no revoke-routing skew).

Implementing:

# Changelog

All notable changes to `agent-sync` are documented in this file.

The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

While the project is pre-1.0, minor version bumps may include breaking changes;
the adapter wire protocol follows its own "freeze the frame, grow capabilities"
compatibility policy documented in `docs/spec/adapter-protocol-v1.md`.

## [Unreleased]

### Added

- **Zero-flag `init`.** `agent-sync init` no longer requires a source or
  targets: with no source flag the canonical source defaults to the in-repo
  `.agents` directory (created when missing, so the first sync soft-lands on
  the zero-emit hint instead of a missing-source error), and with no
  `--target` the target list is **discovered** from the workspace's tool
  footprints (`.claude/` → claude, `.cursor/` → cursor, `.codex/` → codex,
  `.pi/` → pi, `.agent/` → antigravity) and snapshotted into `targets:`,
  sorted. Zero footprints still writes the manifest (empty `targets:` is the
  spec-valid "not yet configured" state) with a hint naming `--target`. The
  success line announces every inference —
  `wrote .agent-sync.yaml (source: .agents [default]; targets: claude [discovered])` —
  and detected-but-not-enabled footprints when explicit `--target` flags
  override discovery. PATH adapters are never auto-discovered.

54	### T1 — Compromised or malicious canonical source (supply chain)
55	An attacker gains push access to the canonical repo, or MITMs the fetch, and
56	serves malicious content (e.g. a `rule` or `mcp-server-entry` that exfiltrates
57	secrets once an agent loads it).
58	
59	**Mitigations.**
60	- **Pinning is default** (invariant #4): `init` resolves refs to a SHA and writes
61	  it back. A moving branch cannot silently change what syncs.
62	- **`trusted_sha:`** is the authoritative integrity anchor in non-interactive/CI
63	  contexts: sync **fails closed** on a mismatch, with no prompt. The pin is a
64	  value a human code-reviewed into the repo, mirroring `go.sum` / lockfile
65	  precedent.
66	- **TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`** (B3): first use of an unseen pair requires an explicit
67	  interactive decision; CI never auto-accepts.
68	- Git fetches verify the resolved SHA matches the requested pin before the commit
69	  is admitted to cache (B1).
70	
71	**Residual risk.** agent-sync does **not** verify upstream commit signatures
72	(e.g. GPG/SSH-signed tags) in v1. A maliciously authored commit that a user
73	pins and trusts will sync. Defense relies on the human review that produced the
74	pin. Commit-signature verification is a candidate for a future minor version.

file.content:
### T1 — Compromised or malicious canonical source (supply chain)
An attacker gains push access to the canonical repo, or MITMs the fetch, and
serves malicious content (e.g. a `rule` or `mcp-server-entry` that exfiltrates
secrets once an agent loads it).

**Mitigations.**
- **Pinning is default** (invariant #4): `init` resolves refs to a SHA and writes
  it back. A moving branch cannot silently change what syncs.
- **`trusted_sha:`** is the authoritative integrity anchor in non-interactive/CI
  contexts: sync **fails closed** on a mismatch, with no prompt. The pin is a
  value a human code-reviewed into the repo, mirroring `go.sum` / lockfile
  precedent.
- **TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`** (B3): first use of an unseen pair requires an explicit
  interactive decision; CI never auto-accepts.
- Git fetches verify the resolved SHA matches the requested pin before the commit
  is admitted to cache (B1).

**Residual risk.** agent-sync does **not** verify upstream commit signatures
(e.g. GPG/SSH-signed tags) in v1. A maliciously authored commit that a user
pins and trusts will sync. Defense relies on the human review that produced the
pin. Commit-signature verification is a candidate for a future minor version.

content.text: Now I'll update T1 to reflect the posture flip and the rails that replace human review on the default path.

input.old_string:
**Mitigations.**
- **Pinning is default** (invariant #4): `init` resolves refs to a SHA and writes
  it back. A moving branch cannot silently change what syncs.
- **`trusted_sha:`** is the authoritative integrity anchor in non-interactive/CI
  contexts: sync **fails closed** on a mismatch, with no prompt. The pin is a
  value a human code-reviewed into the repo, mirroring `go.sum` / lockfile
  precedent.
- **TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`** (B3): first use of an unseen pair requires an explicit
  interactive decision; CI never auto-accepts.
- Git fetches verify the resolved SHA matches the requested pin before the commit
  is admitted to cache (B1).

**Residual risk.** agent-sync does **not** verify upstream commit signatures
(e.g. GPG/SSH-signed tags) in v1. A maliciously authored commit that a user
pins and trusts will sync. Defense relies on the human review that produced the
pin. Commit-signature verification is a candidate for a future minor version.
input.new_string:
**Mitigations.**
- **The manifest always carries a concrete pin.** `init` resolves refs to a SHA
  and writes it back; auto-advance (below) re-pins **forward** to a new concrete
  SHA rather than floating. A moving branch never silently changes what syncs —
  every synced state is a recorded commit.
- **Auto-advance is fast-forward-only.** By default `sync` advances the pin to the
  newest upstream commit, but only when the new SHA is a descendant of the current
  `trusted_sha`. A rewritten / force-pushed history is **not** a fast-forward and
  is refused (the pin is kept; the scope is reported). This is the primary rail
  now that human review is not in the default advance path.
- **Auto-advance routes through the trust policy engine, not around it.** The
  allow-new-shas posture is evaluated by `internal/trust` `Decide`: an active
  **revoke always wins** (even under auto), and the posture is **fail-closed
  without loaded trust state** — a caller that has not folded the trust store
  cannot auto-advance. Every auto-advance appends to `pending.jsonl` for audit.
- **`trusted_sha:`** remains the authoritative integrity anchor: with auto-advance
  disabled (`--frozen` / `canonical.auto: false`) sync **fails closed** on a
  mismatch in non-interactive/CI, with no prompt — the human-code-reviewed,
  `go.sum`-like behavior, preserved for reproducibility-sensitive workspaces.
- **TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`** (B3): first use of an unseen pair requires an explicit
  interactive decision; CI never auto-accepts.
- Git fetches verify the resolved SHA matches the requested pin before the commit
  is admitted to cache (B1); offline / unreachable sync falls back to the cached
  pin rather than adopting an unverified head.

**Residual risk.** With auto-advance as the default, **human review is no longer
in the default path** for moving the pin forward — fast-forward-only and the
trust policy (revoke + fail-closed) are the remaining rails. agent-sync still does
**not** verify upstream commit signatures (e.g. GPG/SSH-signed tags), so a
malicious but fast-forward commit pushed to the tracked ref by someone with push
access **will** auto-advance and sync unless the anchor is revoked or the
workspace opts out via `--frozen` / `canonical.auto: false`. Commit-signature
verification is now a higher-value candidate for a future version. Workspaces that
require human-reviewed advancement should set `canonical.auto: false` and move the
pin with the explicit, gated `agent-sync update`.

content.text: Now the CHANGELOG — a breaking-change entry with a migration note. Let me insert a `### Changed` section under `[Unreleased]`.

input.old_string:
## [Unreleased]

### Added
input.new_string:
## [Unreleased]

### Changed

- **BREAKING (semver-major): `agent-sync sync` auto-advances the canonical pin by
  default.** Previously `sync` materialized the pinned `commit` and never moved it;
  advancing required the explicit, gated `agent-sync update`. Now `sync` resolves
  the newest upstream commit on the manifest's `ref`, and — for git-backed sources
  that are pinned and carry a `trusted_sha` — advances the pin and re-pins
  `commit` + `trusted_sha` forward automatically, auto-accepting the trust
  decision **through the trust policy engine**. Safety rails: advancement is
  **fast-forward-only** (rewritten/force-pushed history is refused, pin kept); a
  **revoked trust anchor always refuses**; **offline / unreachable falls back to
  the cached pin** with a warning (never errors); every advance is recorded to the
  structured log and the cache audit file. `local_dir` sources are unaffected
  (nothing to pin); `local_path` sources advance fast-forward-only.

  **Migration.** On upgrade, existing pinned workspaces begin tracking upstream on
  the next `sync`. To keep today's deterministic, human-gated behavior:
  - pass `--frozen` for a single run, or
  - set `canonical.auto: false` in `.agent-sync.yaml` to opt a workspace out
    permanently (then advance deliberately with `agent-sync update`).

  CI and any reproducibility-sensitive pipeline should use one of the opt-outs.
  See `docs/threat-model.md` (T1) for the revised supply-chain posture: human
  review is no longer in the default advance path, so fast-forward-only and the
  trust policy are the remaining rails, and commit-signature verification is still
  absent.

### Added
